Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: First, I need to understand the raw log data. Looking at the timestamps and events, there are multiple applications and documents being interacted with over a short period. The key challenge is defining meaningful cases - logical units of work that will make sense for process analysis.

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: I notice the user switches between Word documents, an email client, a PDF viewer, and Excel. There are two distinct Word documents: Quarterly_Report.docx and Document1.docx. The email interaction seems self-contained. Excel has its own budget update task. The PDF viewing might be related to reviewing something.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: For case grouping, Im thinking the document-centric approach makes the most sense. Each document or major task could be a case. Document1.docx seems to have multiple interactions - initial drafting, then later adding budget references. The email thread is clearly one unit. Quarterly_Report.docx appears twice separately. Excel has its own sequence.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Activity naming should be standardized rather than using raw actions like TYPING or FOCUS. Something like Edit Document, Review Email, Update Spreadsheet would be more meaningful for analysis. The timestamps and application context help determine these activity types.

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Ill structure the output with a clear case ID, descriptive activity names, and the original timestamps. The explanation should cover why I chose these particular case groupings and activity names, especially how I handled the document switches and task boundaries.
